text: Hey guys, it's Dr. Kajan Singh. Today we are going to watch a small clip of me doing a laparoscopic appendectomy. It's a short clip but it will give you an idea how do I do appendix surgery laparoscopically. These are my port size, twelve millimeters belly button and two five millimeter on the left side. So here we are in the abdominal cavity or belly or peritoneal cavity.
The laparoscopic view shows the cecum and appendix, with visible vessels supplying the appendix and the appendix mesentery. The small bowel is seen ending into the colon.

The surgeon creates a window in the mesentery of the appendix using a conventional method. The blood vessels are separated to one side, with the cecum visible on the opposite side. A stapler is used to divide the mesentery.

The surgeon uses a stapler with a vascular load to divide the appendix vessels, firing three rows of staples on each side and cutting between them. The stapler appears white, and the thin appendix is stapled and divided in one action.
The surgeon uses a blue vascular tissue load stapler to divide the appendix at its junction with the cecum. The appendix is then placed into a small retrieval bag for removal.

The surgeon places the appendix into a retrieval bag with metal prongs and a purse string closure, securing the bag before removing it from the abdominal cavity. If bleeding occurs, clips can be applied to control it.
